description: Search response field-name conformance. The wrapping array MUST be named `matches`. Registries MUST NOT emit `results` (or any other alternative spelling). Conformant consumers MUST reject responses that lack `matches`. The schema is additionalProperties: false; a response containing `results` is non-conformant by schema even before the prose check.

scenarios.name: consumer-side: consumer MUST treat `results` as non-conformant

expected.consumer_behavior: Conformant consumer MUST treat the response as non-conformant. Acceptable handling: surface a parse/schema error to the caller, OR return zero matches. Consumer MUST NOT silently coerce `results` to `matches`.
expected.rationale: Silent coercion would mask registry bugs and erode the schema contract.

scenarios.name: consumer-side: RECOMMENDED diagnostic when `results` is detected (RFC-ACDP-0005 §2.2)

expected.consumer_behavior: Consumer SHOULD surface an observable diagnostic — log warning, structured event, OpenTelemetry span attribute, or equivalent — explicitly stating that the response contained a `results` key instead of `matches`. The diagnostic MUST be observable to integration testers WITHOUT enabling debug-level logging across the stack. The functional outcome remains the same as the previous scenario (parse/schema error or zero matches); the diagnostic is added to disambiguate 'registry returned zero matches' from 'registry used the wrong field name'.
expected.rationale: Without an explicit diagnostic, a consumer that silently degrades to zero-match handling makes registry field-name bugs indistinguishable from genuine empty result sets. Operators chasing 'why is search returning nothing' would have to packet-capture or attach a debugger; the SHOULD-level diagnostic is the cheapest way to surface the bug at the right layer.
minimum_diagnostic_content: The HTTP path that produced the response (e.g. `GET /contexts/search?q=...`).
The actual top-level keys observed (so operators can also detect other misnamings like `records`/`items`).
A reference to RFC-ACDP-0005 §2.2 / fixture vis-003.

background: A reference implementation accidentally serialized the array under `results`. Because no behavioral fixture pinned the field name, the bug went unnoticed until a cross-impl interop test failed.
